coredata.dc:title: Bactericidal laser ablation of carbon dots: An in vitro study on wild-type and antibiotic-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Sattarahmady, N.
Rezaie-Yazdi, M.
Tondro, G.H.
Akbari, N.
coredata.dc:description: Suspensions of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) were irradiated with a 808-nm diode laser in the presence of different concentrations of carbon dots (C-dots). The effects of laser irradiation and C-dots on the bactericidal activity were separately investigated. The results indicated that without laser irradiation, C-dots caused to a significant cease in the colony formation of both wild and methicillin-resistance types of S. aureus in a concentration-dependent manner. On the other hand, the bactericidal effect of C-dots was accelerated upon laser irradiation. Mechanistic studies revealed that formation of reactive oxygen species and protein leakage, which occurred in the presence of C-dots, were greatly accelerated by laser irradiation. Moreover, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images showed morphological changes in the bacterial surfaces in the presence of C-dots upon laser irradiations, while almost no changes in the bacteria morphology were observed in the presence of C-dots alone. Outer membrane damage was a synergism result of laser irradiation and presence of C-dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal therapy
Microbial resistance
Photoabsorbent agent
Antimicrobial activity
Carbon dots
Staphylococcus aureus

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal Effect of C-dots Upon Laser Irradiation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM (A, B) and TEM (C, D, and E) images of C-dot with different magnifications, and a UV–vis spectrum of C-dots (F).

Temperature increment dependencies of C-dots mixtures of different concentrations of 1.7, 3.4, 7.0 and 14.0μgmL−1 dispersed in BHI broth containing bacteria of 5×108 CFUmL−1 during laser irradiation with two energy densities of 0.36 (A) and 0.84kJcm−2 (B).
CFUmL−1 for S. aureus and MRSA groups after treatment by two laser energy densities of 0.36 (A) and 0.84kJcm−2 (B).
Absorbance at 595nm of extracellular media of all groups of bacterial cells showing the protein release content based on Bradford assay upon laser irradiation at 0.36 (A) and 0.84kJcm−2 (B).

Fluorescence intensities of different groups of S. aureus, after interaction with DCFH-DA. The data is presented as panels A–D to compare the effects of laser irradiation and its energy, and C-dots and its concentration.

Fluorescence intensity of the samples of all groups of MRSA, after interaction with DCFH-DA. The data is presented as panels A–D to compare the effects of laser irradiation and its energy, and C-dots and its concentration.
FESEM images for S. aureus. (A) [L− C−], (B) [L+(0.84) C−], (C) [L+(0.36) C+(1.7)].
FESEM images for MRSA. (A) [L− C−], (B) [L+(0.84) C−], (C) [L+(0.36) C+(1.7)].
Experimental conditions of bacteria treatment by C-dots and laser irradiation.

head.ce:title: Bactericidal laser ablation of carbon dots: An in vitro study on wild-type and antibiotic-resistant Staphylococcus aureus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Physics, School of Medicine, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanomedicine and Nanobiology Research Center, Shiraz University of Medical Sciences, Shiraz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Science, Arak Branch, Islamic Azad University, Arak, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Suspensions of Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) were irradiated with a 808-nm diode laser in the presence of different concentrations of carbon dots (C-dots). The effects of laser irradiation and C-dots on the bactericidal activity were separately investigated. The results indicated that without laser irradiation, C-dots caused to a significant cease in the colony formation of both wild and methicillin-resistance types of S. aureus in a concentration-dependent manner. On the other hand, the bactericidal effect of C-dots was accelerated upon laser irradiation. Mechanistic studies revealed that formation of reactive oxygen species and protein leakage, which occurred in the presence of C-dots, were greatly accelerated by laser irradiation. Moreover, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images showed morphological changes in the bacterial surfaces in the presence of C-dots upon laser irradiations, while almost no changes in the bacteria morphology were observed in the presence of C-dots alone. Outer membrane damage was a synergism result of laser irradiation and presence of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:para: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) as a gram-positive bacterium is found at the natural microflora of various parts of the human and animal bodies [1]. S. aureus causes different diseases in human and animals such as skin and wound infections, enteritis, pneumonia, toxic shock syndrome, and osteomyelitis and implant-associated infections [2,3]. Nowadays, methicillin as the first choice antibiotic against S. aureus has lost its performance due to the emergence of resistant strains [4]. Drug resistance in bacteria causes delayed recovery of wound infection [5]. Genes conferring resistance to the antibiotics have horizontally been transferred in bacteria including the enzymes with degrading drug ability or drug efflux-influx alteration [6]. Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) is the first class of antibiotic-resistant pathogens with serious threat of public health [7]. However, development of new antibiotics is a time-consuming process, and MRSA undergoes rapidly epidemiological development and acquires resistance to the available antibiotics [8]. Other treatments of wound infections, such as surgery removal or antiseptic solutions, have some adverse effects on normal cells or skin inflammation around the target area [9]. Therefore, these strategies are not pleasurable options for infected wounds or burns treatment.

Since the traditional antibacterial strategies using antibiotics have encountered drug resistance, prompt new approaches for the treatment of bacterial infections are required [10]. One of these approaches is photodynamic therapy (PDT) which is based on light absorption of appropriate wavelength with a photosensitizing agent (photosensitizer) [11] and results in the formation of free radicals and/or reactive oxygen species (ROS), irreversible damages in cellular elements and cell death [12]. However, drawbacks of PDT limit its application against bacteria [2]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) of bacteria using nanomaterials and a laser source presents a selective thermal ablation strategy [13], and causes irreversible destruction [13,14]. Although the light of different wavelengths can be employed in PDT and PTT [15,16], because biological entities do not absorb light in the near-infrared (NIR) range, materials of absorbing light in the range of 700–1100nm are desirable in PTT. NIR light penetrates the biological tissues up to several centimeters [17].

Nanomaterials represent different physical and chemical properties, compared to their bulk counterparts, and have potential applications in catalysis and electrocatalysis [18–20], energy-related devices [21], drug design and delivery [22,23], imaging [24,25] and pathogens detection [26,27]. These materials with strong optical absorbance, such as gold nanostructures [28,29], carbonaceous nanomaterials [30], chalcogenide nanoparticles [31], and metal oxide nanostructures [32] have been employed as photosensitizers. These nanostructures convert the NIR light into vibrational energy and generate heat [33].

In recent years, carbon nanomaterials have had important roles in different fields of nanomedicine such as gene delivery [34], fabrication of sensors and biosensors [20,35,36], tissue engineering [37], imaging [34] and theranostics [38]. Carbon dots (C-dots) are a class of quantum dots with a quasi-spherical shape and a property-dependent size [39]. C-dots display advantageous properties such as chemical stability, photostability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, water solubility and eco-friendliness, compared to some other semiconductor quantum dots [40].

The aim of the present study is to investigate the PTT effect of C-dots on the viability of wild and methicillin-resistant strains of S. aureus in vitro. Photoinactivation of C-dots is elucidated by the evaluation of protein leakage, ROS production, and morphology changes of bacteria.
All chemicals were purchased from Sigma Chemicals (USA), Scharlau Chemie (Spain) or Merck (Germany) without further purification.

C-dots were synthesized in a one-pot solution synthesis method [41] with some modifications. Briefly, 0.17g ascorbic acid and 0.02g copper acetate hydrate were dissolved in 20mL water under stirring for 10min at room temperature, and then incubated for 5h at 90°C. The reaction mixture turned into yellow solution after this incubation time. An elemental CHNSO analysis was performed to determine the carbon content of the synthesized sample. The size of and morphology of C-dots were measured using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). FESEM and TEM were performed by a Zeiss, Sigma-IGMA/VP field emission scanning electron microscope (Germany), and a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope (Germany) operating at 80kV, on a carbon formvar-coated copper grid, respectively. UV–vis spectra were recorded by a spectrophotometer Rayleigh 2100 (China).

All materials, vessels and glassware were sterilized by autoclaving at 120°C for 20min. The standard strains of the wild type of S. aureus (PTCC 1931) and MRSA (PTCC 1764) were purchased from Iranian Organization of Science and Technology (Iran) and subcultured on nutrient agar (Scharlau Chemie, Spain). Then, a colony of the bacteria was inoculated in brain heart infusion (BHI) broth (Scharlau Chemie, Spain), incubated in an orbital shaker at 37°C for 24h to reach the middle of logarithmic phase of growth (OD600 =0.3, ≈final concentration of bacteria of 5×108 CFUmL−1) and employed as a bacteria stock [42]. Subsequently, 150μL of bacteria stock was suspended in vials and diluted to 250μL with BHI broth for photothermal treatments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal Effect of C-dots Upon Laser Irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the temperature increment during photothermal treatment, a 808-nm 1.0W CW diode laser from DAJ Co. (Iran) and C-dots were employed as the light source and photothermal agent, respectively. The output power of the diode laser was calibrated using a Lambda optical power meter (Australia). The distance between the laser tip and the illuminated target was regulated to create an effective spot light of 1cm in diameter, with fixed energy densities of 0.36 and 0.84kJcm−2.

0.5-mL mixtures of C-dots of different concentrations (1.7, 3.4, 7.0, 14 and 55μgmL−1) dispersed in BHI broth containing bacteria of 5×108 CFUmL−1 in autoclavable vials were separately exposed to the laser light with energy densities of 0.36 and 0.84kJcm−2. The temperature increments were measured at 0, 20, 30, 40 and 60min of light exposure by a digital thermoprobe from DAJ Co. (Iran). The probe was placed vertically in the center of the samples, approximately 3–5mm deep inside the vials. BHI broth was selected as a control. Each measurement was replicated three times.

Photothermal treatments of the bacteria were investigated in different conditions using different concentrations of C-dots (Table 1 ). Different groups of the bacteria strains were selected as: no laser and no C-dots ([L− C−]); exposure to laser in the presence of C-dots ([L+ C+]); exposure to laser without C-dots ([L+ C−]); and no laser with C-dots ([L− C+]). [L− C−] was selected as a control. For [C+] groups, 250μL of a C-dots solution was added to 250μL of bacterial suspension and mixed by vortex for 1min. For [L+] groups, bacterial suspensions were immediately exposed to laser irradiation for 20min at energy densities of 0.36 and 0.84kJcm−2. For [L−] groups, bacteria suspensions were kept at dark for 21min. For [C−] groups, a bacterial suspension was diluted by 250μL deionized water.

Following by applying conditions mentioned above, the samples were diluted according to the serial dilution method. The diluted samples were plated on tryptic soy agar and incubated in dark at 37°C for 24h. The numbers of CFU grown on the plates were counted and multiplied by the dilution factor to obtain viabilities of the bacteria. Each experiment was repeated three times.

Detection of intracellular ROS generation was performed based on fluorescence detection of dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Samples of bacteria suspensions were centrifuged at 10000rpm for 20min, washed two times with 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.0), mixed with 0.1mM 2′,7′-dichlorodihydro fluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) in a phosphate buffer (pH7.0):ethanol mixture (80:20V/V), incubated for 30min at dark and 37°C, and centrifuged at 10000rpm for 20min. Then, the cells' suspensions were washed by 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.0) and re-suspended by sonication in 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.0) for 10min. After these steps, 100μL of the cell extracts was mixed with 1mL 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.0), and the fluorescence intensity of the samples was measured by a Cary Eclipse spectrofluorescence (USA) at room temperature, with an excitation wavelength of 480nm and an emission wavelength range of 505–550nm.

Cell-cell adhesion and cell membrane damage lead to leakage and release of the intracellular content of bacteria [43]. Protein leakage was detected by the Bradford assay using bovine serum albumin as a standard [44]. Samples of treated bacteria were filtered through 0.4μm-pore size black polycarbonate membranes to remove the bacterial cells, and then the absorbance of the filtered fluids was recorded at 595nm after 3min of incubation at dark based on Bradford assay. The filtered fluid of the cells without treatment was selected as a control, and the contents of leaked protein from the treated bacteria were compared with the control.

Samples of the treated bacteria of [L− C−], [L− C+] and [L+ C+] groups were investigated by FESEM images. For sample preparation, the treated bacteria were centrifuged at 5000rpm for 20min, washed three times with 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.4), and fixed with 2% glutaraldehyde in 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.4) at 4°C for 12h. Then, the bacteria were centrifuged at 5000rpm for 20min and washed three times with 100mM phosphate buffer (pH7.4). Then, the samples were exposed to a 1% osmium tetroxide solution for 30min at room temperature. They were dehydrated in an acetone gradient and the last suspensions of the samples were dropped on a lamella, and the lamella was coated by a very thin gold film by sputtering for FESEM observation. FESEM images of the bacteria were recorded using TESCAN Mira3-XMU (Czech Republic).

The numbers of viable bacteria were presented as mean±standard deviation. The values of CFUmL−1 were analyzed using t-test between different groups using GraphPad software. The level of statistical difference was set at p<0.05.

Fig. 1 shows FESEM (A, B) and TEM (C–E) images of C-dots with different magnifications. C-dots had a spherical shape with an average diameter of 16±2nm (n=50) obtained by FESEM, and 12±1nm (n=3) obtained by TEM. Both FESEM and TEM images confirmed a narrow distribution size of the particles. In addition, the mean zeta potential value of C-dots at working pH (3.49) was obtained as −75±4mV (n=3) arising from deprotonation of the carboxyl groups of C-dots with a pKa value of 4.7. A UV–vis spectrum of C-dots is also presented in Fig. 1(F). The spectrum contained characteristic absorption peaks at 262 and 322nm related to C O and C C bands. In addition, the spectrum showed a broad absorption in a wide range of 600–820nm. Therefore, C-dots can absorb lights of UV and 808-nm wavelengths.

Fig. 2 represents temperature increment dependency of C-dots mixtures with different concentrations of 1.7, 3.4, 7.0 and 14.0μgmL−1 dispersed in BHI broth containing bacteria of 5×108 CFUmL−1 during laser irradiation with two energy densities of 0.36 (A) and 0.84kJcm−2 (B). The results showed that C-dots induced increment in the temperature of the solutions at both energy densities of 0.36 and 0.84kJcm−2. Increment in the temperature had both C-dots concentration and time of light irradiation dependencies. Therefore, C-dots represented suitable photothermal efficiency by the NIR laser irradiation. For further studies, the C-dots concentrations of 3.4 and 7μgmL−1 at the laser energy density of 0.36kJcm−2 and the C-dots concentration of 1.7μgmL−1 at the laser energy density of 0.84kJcm−2 were employed as the middle concentrations of C-dots at lower laser energy, and a low concentration at higher irradiation energy. Moreover, the laser irradiation time of the bacteria samples was set at 20min because the strains employed in this study duplicate at each 24–30min time intervals under laboratory conditions [45], and we aimed at prevent the bacteria meiosis during the laser irradiation.

Fig. 3 represents CFUmL−1 for each group of the two bacterial strains at the two laser energy densities. The control group was [L− C−]. The survival rates for the [L− C−] and [L+ C−] groups were similar, indicating that both bacteria strains were not solely affected by the laser irradiation. In contrast, bacterial survival in the [L+ C+] and [L− C+] groups dramatically decreased, and photothermal therapy with C-dots ([L+ C+]) reduced CFUmL−1, compared to the [L+ C−] and [L− C−] groups for all the tested concentrations. In addition, more bacteria were killed after photothermal treatment in [L+ C+] group, compared to [L− C+] one, except in the presence of 7.0μgmL−1C-dots. There was a significant difference in antibacterial efficiencies between [L+ C+] and [L− C+] groups in the presence of 1.7 and 3.4μgmL−1C-dots. The bacterial growth showed a typical dose-dependent bactericidal effect of C-dots. Statistical analysis confirmed that treatment with different concentrations of C-dots had a significant effect on the bacterial survival (p<0.05). At all concentrations, C-dots significantly inhibited the growth of both the strains, and the bacterial growths were completely inhibited in the presence of 7.0μgmL−1C-dots.

Protein release is a sign of the loss of membrane integrity of the bacteria [46]. Fig. 4 shows the absorption intensities of extracellular media measured at 595nm for all groups of bacteria at the two laser energy densities and the three C-dots concentrations (see also Table 1). This absorption intensity is an indication for protein content of the media. Increment in the protein content in the bacterium media was observed for all the groups treated with C-dots either without ([L− C+]) or with ([L+ C+]) laser irradiation with a dependency on the C-dots concentration. In the absence of C-dots, no protein leakage to the media was observed either without or with laser irradiation. However, laser irradiation led to acceleration of protein release by C-dots. Therefore, laser irradiation and C-dots play a bactericidal role in a synergistic manner. It should be also mentioned that protein release in all groups of S. aureus was higher than MRSA groups.

Bacterial killing can be induced by toxicity effect of oxidative stress. For example, metal nanoparticles result in the ROS formation in cell lines [47]. Intracellular ROS formation can be detected by measuring the increment in the fluorescence intensity of DCF. DCFH-DA penetrates the cell membrane and converts to non-fluorescent 2, 7-dichlorofluorescein (DCFH) via deacetylation by esterases. If ROS is generated, reaction of DCFH with ROS produces the fluorescent by-product DCF in the cells [43]. Fluorescence spectra recorded for the treated groups of bacteria after interaction with DCFH-DA are shown in Figs. 5 and 6
(for S. aureus and MRSA, respectively). The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity increment for [L+ C+] group was higher than [L− C+] one, confirming a higher ROS level generated for the former group. In addition, the fluorescence intensities of [L+ C−] and [L− C−] groups were near the same. Therefore, laser irradiation did not solely affect the ROS formation process. All these results indicate that C-dots had a great effect on the ROS formation in a concentration dependent manner, while laser irradiation enhanced the ROS generation of C-dots by a synergistic effect. ROS triggers membrane damage and induces changes in the membrane permeability, cytoplasmic content leakage [48], and most probably allows the C-dots to penetrate the bacteria cell yielding killing.

For further investigation of the bactericidal laser ablation of C-dots, FESEM images from the bacteria samples were recorded. Figs. 7 and 8
show the FESEM images of the native and treated S. aureus and MRSA. For both the strains, untreated cells appeared bright, round and smooth, and were aggregated and clumped. Treatment of S. aureus with C-dots caused deformation and damages on the cell surface accompanied by changes in the morphology and shape of the cells. Laser irradiation of the treated S. aureus cells by C-dots induced a huge damage to the cell structure and umbilicus formation. In addition, the cell size and volume were dramatically decreased. It seems that cell walls were opened and the cell entity leaked, causing a deep damage to the cells. On the other hand, treatment of MRSA with C-dots caused a deep change in the cell morphology, cell clustering and damages to the cell surface. Laser irradiation of the treated MRSA cells by C-dots induced great changes in the cell morphology, and caused flattening of the cells and draining of the cell content.

S. aureus has an incremented hydrophobicity during the exponential phase of growth, and constant hydrophobicity in the stationary phase [49] with a moderate negative surface charge [50]. S. aureus can adhere to hydrophobic or slightly negative charged surface of C-dots, while the direct interaction of bacteria and the C-dots surface can be controlled by attractive van der Waals forces [51]. Although C-dots had a negative zeta potential of −75±4mV, van der Waals forces could overcome the (weak) repulsion forces [52] and lead to adhesion of C-dots to the cell surface.

The main reasons for the bactericide effect of C-dots were the cell capturing and light absorbing abilities. NIR irradiation of C-dots raised the solution temperature inducing ROS production and cell wall damages. These alterations influence the structure and function of proteins, enzymes and lipids in the bacterial cells [42,53], and subsequently induce bacterial death. The results indicated that photothermal effect of C-dots provided toxicity on S. aureus and MRSA in a concentration dependent manner with cell wall disruption, higher membrane permeability, higher content diffusion and oxidative stress. Photo-ablation strategy represents a new minimally invasive way for flight with bacteria.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The paper has been extracted from the M. Rezaie-Yazdi M.Sc. thesis supported by the Research Council of Shiraz University of Medical Sciences (9746).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotobiol.2016.12.006.
